title: Intel Foundry Nabs A Custom ASIC Win with Fortinet
gist: Intel Foundry won a custom-ASIC design/manufacturing deal for Fortinet's next-gen Security Processor 6 (SP6), pulling a customer away from TSMC in a segment that tangentially competes with Intel's own x86 products -- though the author estimates the actual revenue at stake is a slice of a roughly $2B hardware stream, not a $10B/year win.

insights.claim: Intel and Fortinet announced they will work together on the Fortinet Security Processor 6 (SP6), a new custom ASIC, with Intel providing advanced design, packaging, and manufacturing capabilities and Fortinet providing its security-processor expertise.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: A concrete, disclosable Intel Foundry customer win outside Intel's usual AI-chip customer base.
insights.locator: Intel Foundry Nabs A Custom ASIC Win with Fortinet

insights.claim: This new Intel-fabricated Fortinet SP6 chip is a different product from AMD's SP6 CPU socket used for parts like the AMD EPYC 8005 'Sorano,' a naming collision worth not confusing.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: A practical disambiguation for readers tracking both companies' roadmaps under the same 'SP6' label.

insights.claim: Fortinet has designed its own custom security ASICs for years, including the FortiSP5 and FortiSOC4, which have been found inside FortiGate FG-40F and FG-60F appliances rather than relying on off-the-shelf CPUs.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Establishes Fortinet as an experienced, credible ASIC customer, not a first-time silicon designer -- de-risking the win for Intel Foundry.
insights.locator: Fortinet's history of custom ASIC design

insights.claim: At its SP5 launch, Fortinet directly compared the FortiSP5 ASIC's performance against 'industry-standard CPUs,' which at the time likely meant an Intel Xeon D or Atom processor.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Shows Fortinet has historically benchmarked its custom silicon against Intel's own general-purpose CPUs, underscoring how competitive this space is for Intel.

themes.theme: Why this matters for Intel Foundry

insights.claim: The author believes the prior-generation FortiSP5 was manufactured on TSMC's 7nm process, meaning Intel is winning a customer away from TSMC with this new SP6 deal.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: A specific, named instance of Intel Foundry displacing TSMC as a fabrication partner, a data point for tracking the foundry competition.
insights.quote: I believe the FortiSP5 was a TSMC 7nm processor, so Intel is gaining a customer that was previously TSMC’s.
insights.locator: Author's inference on FortiSP5 process node

insights.claim: The standout significance is not just that Intel won a former TSMC customer, but that it did so in a product category that tangentially competes with Intel's own x86 franchise.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Highlights an internal tension for Intel: its foundry business is now fabricating a chip designed explicitly to compete with Intel's own CPU products.

insights.claim: Fortinet is roughly a 70% services/30% hardware revenue business, and with about $7B in trailing-12-month revenue, that implies roughly $5B is services and only a portion of the remaining hardware revenue -- itself not solely the security ASIC -- is actually at stake in this deal.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Gives investors a revenue-sizing framework to avoid overstating the deal's financial impact on Intel Foundry.

insights.claim: Despite being framed as a big win, this deal does not appear to be worth $10B/year to Intel's Foundry business; instead it looks like capturing one component of a portion of a roughly $2B hardware revenue stream.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Tempers the headline 'big ASIC win' framing with a realistic revenue estimate -- useful for not overweighting the announcement's financial materiality.
insights.quote: this does not seem like a $10B/ year win for Intel’s Foundry business. Instead, this seems like winning a component of a portion of a $2B revenue stream.
